💊 The Pharmacology of Corticosteroids

Professor Ly · Block 5 Pharmacology · Lecture-order notes with color-coded emphasis

Primary / Secondary / Tertiary / Acute adrenal insufficiency Synthetic corticosteroids Potency + equivalent doses MOA + ADME Adverse effects Drug interactions Acute crisis + maintenance Tx
Red = Exam Emphasis / Danger
Blue = Glucocorticoid Focus
Green = Mineralocorticoid / Phys
Orange = Crisis / Treatment
01 🎯 Learning Objectives
Pre-Class LOs
  • Define primary adrenal insufficiency, secondary adrenal insufficiency, and acute adrenal insufficiency.
  • Describe the pharmacodynamics (mechanism of action) of synthetic corticosteroids (glucocorticoids and mineralocorticoid) and pharmacokinetics (ADME) of synthetic glucocorticoids.
Class LOs
  • Differentiate the synthetic corticosteroids with respect to duration of activity, glucocorticoid potency, and mineralocorticoid potency.
  • Describe the pharmacologic effects and adverse effects from usage of synthetic corticosteroids.
  • Describe CYP450 inhibition / induction and additive toxicities.
  • Describe therapeutic management for primary, secondary, and acute adrenal insufficiency.
02 🔬 Introduction
The adrenal cortex produces 3 categories of hormones
  • Glucocorticoids — cortisol
  • Mineralocorticoids — aldosterone
  • Gonadocorticoids — androgen precursor: dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA) → eventually to sex hormones (testosterone and estradiol)
Adrenal Insufficiency
03 ⚠️ Adrenal Insufficiency — Primary vs Secondary vs Tertiary
TypeDefinitionMechanism / PathophysiologyKey Clinical Features
Primary Adrenal Insufficiency Direct damage to the adrenal glands (seen in Addison's disease) leading to hormone deficiency. Damage to the adrenal cortex causes reduced production of cortisol and aldosterone.

High ACTH causes hyperpigmentation.		 Fatigue, Weight loss, Hypotension, Salt craving, Hyperpigmentation
Secondary Adrenal Insufficiency Insufficient ACTH secretion by the pituitary gland leading to cortisol deficiency. Pituitary dysfunction results in inadequate ACTH stimulation.

Aldosterone is usually normal due to renin-angiotensin regulation.		 Fatigue, Weight loss, Hypotension, Typically no hyperpigmentation
Acute Adrenal Insufficiency (Adrenal Crisis) Sudden, life-threatening drop in cortisol levels, often in stressed or undiagnosed patients.

Requires urgent care: treated with Rapid IV hydrocortisone + fluid support.		 An acute decompensation of adrenal function, frequently triggered by stress (infection, trauma, surgery) in patients with adrenal insufficiency.

Might be seen in a patient who is abruptly withdrawn from glucocorticoids.		 Severe hypotension, Shock, Abdominal pain, Vomiting, Confusion
Disease TypeHypothalamusPituitaryFinal Hormone
Primary (gland issue)IncreasedIncreasedDecreased
Secondary (pituitary issue)IncreasedDecreasedDecreased
Tertiary (hypothalamus issue)DecreasedDecreasedDecreased
High-Yield Interpretation
  • Primary affects the final step, so loss of negative feedback makes upstream hormones go up.
  • Secondary affects the pituitary, so pituitary output and the final hormone are both decreased, while the hypothalamus compensates upward.
  • Tertiary is a hypothalamic problem, so the whole downstream axis is decreased.
Tertiary adrenal insufficiency is commonly drug-induced from exogenous glucocorticoid use; aldosterone is usually normal because zona glomerulosa is under the renin-angiotensin system, not ACTH.
Clinical Manifestations
  • Fatigue, lack of energy or stamina, reduced strength
  • Anorexia, weight loss
  • Gastric pain, nausea, vomiting (most common in primary)
  • Myalgia, joint pain
  • Dizziness
  • Salt craving (primary only)
  • Skin hyperpigmentation (primary only)
  • Alabaster-coloured pale skin (secondary only)
  • Low blood pressure, postural hypotension, dehydration
04 🚨 Acute Adrenal Insufficiency (Adrenal Crisis)
Acute adrenal insufficiency is characterized by an acute adrenocortical insufficiency — a true endocrine emergency where death can occur.
Precipitating Scenarios
  • Patient receiving pharmacologic doses of synthetic glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid requirements are not met.
  • Previously undiagnosed primary adrenal insufficiency + serious infection or other acute major stress (e.g., surgery, trauma).
  • Known primary adrenal insufficiency + patient does not take more glucocorticoid during infection / major illness.
  • Most common: abrupt withdrawal from supraphysiologic doses or chronic glucocorticoid usage when the HPA axis is suppressed.
Clinical Presentation + Diagnosis
  • Severe weakness and fatigue
  • Abdominal or flank pain
  • Severe dehydration → hypotension and shock (circulatory collapse)
  • Hypovolemia may not respond to IV hydration and may require vasopressors
  • Tachycardia
  • Nausea, vomiting, fever, confusion
  • Hypoglycemia
  • Rapid ACTH stimulation test
Synthetic Corticosteroids
05 💉 Synthetic Corticosteroids — Duration, Potency, Equivalent Doses
CorticosteroidDurationGC PotencyMC PotencyEquiv. Dose (mg)Key Notes
Hydrocortisone (Cortisol)Short (8-12 hrs)1120 mgUsed for adrenal insufficiency due to balanced GC and MC activity.
Prednisone (prodrug)Intermediate (12-36 hrs)40.85 mgProdrug converted to prednisolone in the liver; commonly used for inflammatory and autoimmune disorders.
Prednisolone (active)Intermediate (12-36 hrs)40.85 mgActive form of prednisone; often preferred in patients with liver disease.
MethylprednisoloneIntermediate (12-36 hrs)50.54 mgLess mineralocorticoid effect than prednisone; commonly used in asthma, MS, lupus.
TriamcinoloneIntermediate (12-36 hrs)504 mgNo mineralocorticoid effects; preferred for rheumatologic conditions.
BetamethasoneLong (36-72 hrs)2500.75 mgPotent anti-inflammatory; used in fetal lung maturation.
DexamethasoneLong (36-72 hrs)2500.75 mgHighly potent, long-acting; used in cerebral edema and Cushing's syndrome diagnosis.
FludrocortisoneIntermediate (12-36 hrs)10125Used only for MC effectsUsed in adrenal insufficiency and orthostatic hypotension due to strong Na⁺ retention.
Quick anchor: hydrocortisone 20 mg = prednisone 5 mg = prednisolone 5 mg = methylprednisolone 4 mg = triamcinolone 4 mg = dexamethasone 0.75 mg.
06 ⚗️ Pharmacodynamics (MOA) + Pharmacokinetics (ADME)
Glucocorticoids

MOA of synthetic drugs is just like natural cortisol.

Binds to glucocorticoid receptors (GRs) in the cytoplasm of target cells → forms complex that goes into the nucleus and regulates gene expression by either activating or inhibiting transcription.

  1. Steroid binds to CBG in blood.
  2. Once it gets into the cell, the receptor is bound to heat shock protein(s).
  3. Once steroid binds receptor, HSP falls off; now a dimer forms.
  4. Dimer enters nucleus → binds GRE → alters transcription → alters proteins → effect.
Mineralocorticoids

Aldosterone is the most important endogenous mineralocorticoid (salt-retaining activity).

Acts through the intracellular mineralocorticoid receptor and regulates fluids / electrolytes such as Na, K, and H ions.

Binds to mineralocorticoid receptors (MRs) in the kidney, colon, and sweat glands → increases Na⁺ reabsorption and K⁺ excretion by upregulating Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase and ENaC in the distal tubules.

Activate mineralocorticoid receptors, leading to sodium and water retention.

ADME of Synthetic Glucocorticoids
  • Absorption: generally well absorbed after oral administration
  • Distribution: wide distribution to muscles, liver, skin, intestines, kidneys, etc.
  • Metabolism: primarily metabolized in the liver by CYP450 enzymes
  • Prednisone (prodrug) → prednisolone (active metabolite)
  • Excretion: excreted mainly via the kidneys as urine
  • When quick onset of action is needed, use intravenous administration.
Effects & Adverse Effects
07 ⚡ Effects of Glucocorticoids + Adverse Effects
EffectPharmacologic MechanismAdverse EffectsNotes
Anti-inflammatory & ImmunosuppressiveDownregulate pro-inflammatory genes and cytokines; upregulate anti-inflammatory proteins; reduce leukocyte migration.Increased risk of infections, Immunosuppression (leukocytosis - WBCs up), Masks signs of infectionEssential for inflammatory disorders but predisposes to opportunistic infections.
Metabolic (Glucose & Protein)Enhance gluconeogenesis in the liver; decrease peripheral glucose uptake, increase proteolysis.Hyperglycemia, Cushingoid appearance (central obesity, buffalo hump)Helpful in stress but adverse when prolonged — especially in diabetic patients.
Bone EffectsInhibit osteoblast activity and increase osteoclast-mediated bone resorption.Osteoporosis, fractures, decreased bone densityLong-term therapy is a well-known risk factor for bone loss.
HPA Axis SuppressionExert negative feedback on the HPA axis, reducing endogenous cortisol production.Adrenal suppression and atrophy, risk of acute adrenal insufficiency if abruptly discontinued (must take patient off drug slowly)Critical to taper corticosteroids rather than stopping them suddenly.
Mineralocorticoid Effects (CV)Some corticosteroids activate mineralocorticoid receptors, leading to sodium and water retention.Edema, Hypertension, Electrolyte imbalances (hypokalemia, hypernatremia)Useful for replacing aldosterone but harmful when excessive.
Other Systemic EffectsCorticosteroids affect multiple systems beyond their primary actions.GI disturbances (peptic ulcers, GI bleeding), Increased bleeding, Skin thinning, Cataracts, glaucoma, Neuropsychiatric issuesEmphasizes need for careful dose management and monitoring.
High-Yield Anti-Inflammatory Mechanisms
  • Transactivation: expression of anti-inflammatory and regulator proteins
  • Anti-inflammatory proteins: IL-10, Annexin A1, IκB
  • Transrepression: suppressed synthesis of pro-inflammatory proteins
  • Suppressed mediators: IL-1, IL-2, IL-6, IL-8, COX-2, prostaglandins, TNF, IFN-γ
High-Yield Cell-Count / Immune-Cell Effects
  • Neutrophil count increases
  • Lymphocytes, eosinophils, and basophils decrease via promoted apoptosis
  • Dendritic-cell maturation is inhibited
  • Macrophage behavior shifts with dose effects
🔑Mineralocorticoid vs Glucocorticoid Adverse Effects
Mineralocorticoid AEs
Edema · Hypokalemia · Hypernatremia · Hypertension
Glucocorticoid AEs
Hyperglycemia · Peptic Ulcer · Fractures · Osteoporosis · Glaucoma · Adrenal suppression · Immunosuppression · Depression · Insomnia · Mood swings
Other Major Side Effects
Heart failure · HTN · Gastritis · GI bleed · Pancreatitis · Depression · Insomnia · Mania/psychosis · Hypokalemia · Fluid volume shifts · Acne · Hirsutism · Hypertrichosis · Skin thinning/purpura · Amenorrhea · Infertility · Cataract · Glaucoma · Myopathy · Increased appetite · Weight gain · Stroke
08 💊 Drug / Drug Interactions of Glucocorticoids
💡
CYP3A is the anchor.
CYP3A Inducers

Decrease serum concentration → decreased efficacy.

  • Nafcillin
  • Rifampin
  • St. John's wort
CYP3A Inhibitors

Increase serum concentration → increased toxicity.

  • Clarithromycin
Also know: Which corticosteroid is not affected to a clinically relevant degree by this interaction?
Additive Toxicities / Other
  • Warfarin
  • Furosemide, HCTZ
  • Ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin
  • Metformin and insulin
  • Ibuprofen / NSAIDs
  • Oral antacids (aluminum hydroxide, calcium carbonate, magnesium hydroxide)
09 🧪 Mineralocorticoid Focus: Fludrocortisone
Mechanism
  • Aldosterone = most important endogenous mineralocorticoid (salt-retaining activity)
  • Regulates fluids and electrolytes such as Na, K, and H ions
  • Acts through the intracellular mineralocorticoid receptor
  • Binding in the kidney results in potassium excretion and sodium + water reabsorption
  • Fludrocortisone = synthetic corticosteroid and commonly prescribed mineralocorticoid
Expected Adverse Effects
  • Edema
  • Hypokalemia
  • Hypernatremia
  • Hypertension
  • Depression
  • Insomnia
  • Mood swings
  • Hyperglycemia
  • Peptic Ulcer
  • Fractures
  • Osteoporosis
  • Glaucoma
  • Adrenal suppression
  • Immunosuppression
Treatment
10 🩺 Treatment — Acute Crisis vs Chronic Replacement
Acute Adrenal Crisis (Emergency Tx)
  • Parenteral glucocorticoids
  • IV hydrocortisone (interferes with ACTH stimulation test)
  • IV dexamethasone (does not interfere with ACTH stimulation test)
  • Fluid replacement: IV normal saline or IV dextrose 5% in normal saline (D5NS)
  • Mineralocorticoid replacement? Fludrocortisone acetate?
  • Counseling
Maintenance Therapy
  • Agents of choice: hydrocortisone or prednisone
  • Majority of the dose (67%) is given in the morning with the remainder (33%) given 6 to 8 hours later.
  • In primary adrenal insufficiency, fludrocortisone acetate can be used to supplement mineralocorticoid loss.
  • Fludrocortisone may be unnecessary because glucocorticoids, at large doses, can also bind mineralocorticoid receptors.
  • Counsel patients on expected treatment outcomes, consequences of missed doses, and drug side effects.
Case Checkpoint #1
Long-term prednisone + abrupt discontinuation = think HPA-axis suppression with risk of acute adrenal insufficiency.
Case Checkpoint #2
Hyperpigmentation + hyponatremia + hyperkalemia + acidosis + abnormal ACTH stimulation test = think primary adrenal insufficiency / Addison's disease.
